title: Serial killer pore investigation: protocol for analyzing fatigue failure pores in additively manufactured Ti6Al4V
description: {"blocks":[{"key":"i0p3","text":"This protocol outlines the steps followed to determine a potential \"killer pore\" in the area of failure of DMLS Ti6Al4V specimen. ","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"avj66","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"86sbf","text":"The methodology can be used more generally for positively identifying and quantifying small isolated pores in additively manufactured metals. In this case the focus was on analysis of a 4 mm region near the eventual fracture surface, to identify all pores in the vicinity of the \"killer pore\" - the one causing failure by fatigue tests. This protocol was developed for Ti6Al4V with 99.99% density (only small amount of porosity, very small pores only), tensile fatigue specimen round dogbone geometry with 5 mm gauge width.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}}],"entityMap":{}}
guidelines: {"blocks":[{"key":"cg8tm","text":"The samples are scanned before fatigue testing. ","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"7ql5e","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"9iaer","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"e4kls","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"c83ac","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"d3v13","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}}],"entityMap":{}}
before_start: {"blocks":[{"key":"erks7","text":"Make sure to scan with the sample number facing up to know which side is up during analysis. ","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}}],"entityMap":{}}
steps: Wrap the sample inside a bubble wrap for support. Make sure the marked threaded grip is facing up.This is to know which side is up when analysing.
Load the sample on a foam (e.g. florist oasis).
The sample was scanned using the scanned using Phoenix V|Tome|X L240 (General Electric Sensing and Inspection Technologies Phoenix X-ray, Wunstorff, Germany) microCT system. %s %s
Reconstruction of the 3D data is done using General Electric Datos 2.2, using specifically beam hardening correction value of 8 - this reduces beam hardening artefacts significantly.
Analysis of 3D data is described here using Volume Graphics VGSTUDIO MAX 3.2 .
Register the sample - use simple registration to turn the sample upright in the software windows.
De-noising is done using the filters Adaptive Gauss with smoothing factor 0. 8, edge threshold=0.1, iteration=1 and create a new volume.
Perfom the poroity analysis using Probability 0 minimum voxel 8 Maximum voxel 400 Auto threshold deviation Deviation factor Select the edge distance calculation
Use the ellipse Region of interest to crop the sample excluding the area with the cone beam artefact.
Extract the region of interest to create new volume.
Define the material by using the automatic and advanced mode surface determination.
In the area of failure crop out 4 mm
Denoise using the non local mean filter using the smothing factor 2
Define the material by usinng surface determination. Use the automatic and advanced mode.
Sort by minimum edge distance to clearly define the potential crack intiating pores closest to the surface. 3D rotation video_1.wmv 3D rotation video_2.wmv
Save the defect analysis as csv file.
The data is saved including the filtered data set, as a image stack (tiff). These can be viewed manually without special software. The analyzed volume can be opened in free myVGL software.
creator: Muofhe Tshibalanganda
